## [00:00 - 03:01]

Usually the ones where we're spending most of our time to make our decisions going forward is going to be your performance and your conversions and leads. Usually for a report to appointment funnel, I'm looking, I want that click through rate to be around 1%. Now if it's below 0.7%, I know there's a big issue. Now that's one item that you're going to be looking at. But what we found with leads here as well, 407 you see, sometimes the LinkedIn reporting, while it's getting better, it's improving. It's not 100% of the way there. So I will always be checking my lead gen forms. This is where you can actually download a list of anyone that's opted in for your report. ## [06:02 - 09:05]

I think this is important. Once you've actually launched a campaign to know what do I adjust? With bidding, one of the things I like to start people with is LinkedIn, when you set up your ad, is going to give you a suggested bid range. It might say, most people target in this audience are spending between $7 to $30 a click or whatever it may be. Typically what we'll say, our rule of thumb there, is to go about $1 over the minimum suggested bid. If it says it's between $7 and $30, that's a big gap. If it said that, I'd bid somewhere around $8 at the starting point and just see what I get from there. Each day I'm going to be checking to make sure that my budget is being fulfilled, or is at least within 10 to 15% of my budget being fulfilled. Now, one caveat I want to put there in terms of your budget being fulfilled, LinkedIn ads across the board with every client we've had outperform the weekends, sorry, the weekends underperform what happens during the work week. The working hours, when we see the best results on LinkedIn ads. So keep that in mind if you launched an ad on a Friday afternoon or a Saturday morning. I'm not expecting a huge return on people booking appointments on that Saturday and Sunday. I really would prefer launching that on a Monday so I can see a couple days during the work week to see how things are performed before I make any big decisions. But again, is my budget being fulfilled? If not, my bid is probably a little bit too low. I need to wrap that up or I need to look at my audience and see how can I change this. How can I include some more people, switch up some ad copy or creative to make sure it's showing something new to the people that might have already seen this before. For smaller audiences as a general rule of thumb, typically they're going to charge you a little bit more. If the more exclusive you get, they're going to charge you more for that. Now, where that's different can be with sponsored in mail. So if you have a really small audience, a few hundred people or 1,000, 1,500, 2,000 people, you might be looking there as a starting point, look at sponsored in mail to keep your costs in mind. If you're only targeting 2,000 people, there's only 2,000 people that can buy it, sponsored content is likely going to be a high bid to reach those people. So if you have a really high ticket service in the back end, that might be worth it for you, but something to keep in mind. All right, so I want to look at the optimization time on the things that I'm doing daily when I launch a new campaign. As I said earlier, keeping a close eye on lead form submissions, you can either download those leads, or if you set up a little zap through Zapier, you can look specifically at the people coming through in real time who are filling out that information. So daily, I'm also looking at our appointments we're looking from our campaigns. So I can say, here's how much return we're getting on this ad versus this other, and I can make sure my costs are in line for what I'm expecting to pay per appointment. but the marketing companies I'm targeting aren't, how do I put more money to the IT side, and how do I change or refocus or slow the spend on the marketing side? Is what they're selling and their closing percentage going to add up so that they're not just out there spending money on LinkedIn to spend money? We're talking about specific campaigns, direct marketing that's looking to drive someone into an opportunity to purchase whatever it is that you're selling. So we'll look at with clients, we'll say, OK, what is the lifetime value of a client that you have? And what does that look like in terms of ROI for your lifetime value? It doesn't make sense for you to spend $100 to $250 per an appointment for that. It's the biggest thing because, again, where most people come to LinkedIn with, LinkedIn as is saying, I've heard it's expensive and I tried a little bit of something that didn't quite work.
